subject: Neurology
title: Syncope
preambleMarkdown:
# Syncope



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Syncope is a symptom, syndrome, or diagnostic approach topic in neurology.
- This article focuses on localization, neurologic examination, imaging, acute emergencies, rehabilitation, and recurrence prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Syncope to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Syncope is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, progressive, or episodic presentation.
- Localized versus systemic syndrome.
- Stable outpatient problem versus emergency presentation.
- Primary disease, complication, medication effect, functional disorder, or psychosocial contributor.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Syncope depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In neurology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Syncope.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Syncope disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Syncope should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Syncope first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Syncope, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define onset, timing, triggers, severity, associated symptoms, prior episodes, medication exposures, and functional impact.
- The same symptom can represent benign disease or a time-sensitive emergency.
- Vital signs and general appearance often decide whether broad testing or immediate stabilization comes first.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Syncope, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused neurology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Syncope presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Syncope with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Syncope is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Syncope should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize life-threatening causes, treat pain or distress, and choose tests that separate dangerous from common causes.
- Treat the identified cause rather than the symptom label alone.
- Arrange follow-up and safety-net instructions when initial evaluation is nondiagnostic.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Syncope is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses separating common causes from time-sensitive dangerous causes.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Syncope, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Syncope may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Syncope patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Syncope, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Syncope in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Syncope, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Syncope does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Syncope may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Syncope, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Syncope as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- A symptom is not a diagnosis.
- First rule out unstable and cannot-miss causes.
- The next best test is the one that changes the next decision.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Syncope draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Syncope

_Source lines: 5550-5752._

```text
Syncope
A. General Characteristics
1. Syncope refers to a transient loss of consciousness/postural tone
secondary to acute decrease in cerebral blood flow. It is
characterized by rapid recovery of consciousness without
resuscitation.

2. Seizure is not a cause of syncope, it produces acute encephalopathy
but by a different mechanism.

Quick HIT
Prognosis of syncope is generally good, unless cardiac disease is
the underlying cause.

B. Causes

## 1. Cardiac

a. Cardiac syncope is usually sudden and without prodromal
symptoms-for example, the patient's face hits the floor.

b. A cardiac cause of syncope is the most important to consider
and rule out, since syncope may be the first manifestation of a
life-threatening cardiac condition.

c. Causes
- Arrhythmias (e.g., sick sinus syndrome, ventricular
tachycardia, AV block, rapid supraventricular tachycardia).

- Obstruction of blood flow (AS, HCM, pulmonary HTN, atrial
myxoma, etc.).

- PE can theoretically cause sudden obstruction of blood flow
and temporary cerebral hypoperfusion, leading to syncope.

The PESIT trial in 2016 found that for high-risk patients
admitted to the hospital for syncope, one in six patients
were found to have a PE (though this shows correlation, not
causation).

2. Vasovagal syncope ("neurocardiogenic," or "reflex syncope")
a. Most common cause of syncope; may account for up to 50%
of all cases of syncope
b. Most people have one episode, but for some it is a recurrent
problem
c. Clues to diagnosis

- Emotional stress, pain, fear, extreme fatigue, or
claustrophobic situations as precipitating factors.

- Premonitory symptoms (pallor, diaphoresis, lightheadedness,
nausea, dimming of vision, roaring in the ears).

- Can occur at any age, but if the first episode is after age 40,
be reluctant to make this diagnosis.

- Tilt-table study can reproduce the symptoms in susceptible
people.

h. Pathophysiology
- Normally, standing up causes blood to pool in the lower
extremities (leading to a decrease in cardiac output, stroke
volume, and BP). These changes are compensated for by
increased sympathetic tone (leading to vasoconstriction and
tachycardia), and decreased parasympathetic tone.

- In patients with vasovagal syncope, the compensatory
response is interrupted in a few minutes by a paradoxical
withdrawal of sympathetic stimulation and a replacement by
enhanced parasympathetic (vagal) activity. This leads to an
inappropriate bradycardia, vasodilation, marked decrease in
BP, and cerebral perfusion.

k. Treatment
- Can usually be reversed by assuming the supine posture and
elevating the legs.

- beta-Blockers and disopyramide.

- Prognosis is excellent (there is no heart disease or
arrhythmias).

o. Prevention-avoid circumstances that precipitate attack
Quick HIT
Vasovagal and orthostatic syncope occur when the patient is
upright (sitting up or standing). They do not occur when the patient
is recumbent.

1. Orthostatic hypotension
a. Causes include intravascular volume depletion, autonomic
insuficiency (e.g., primary autonomic insuficiency from
Parkinson disease, or secondary from diabetes), age-related loss
of vasomotor reflexes, and drugs.

b. Common in elderly people; diabetics (autonomic neuropathy);
patients taking ganglionic-blocking agents, vasodilators,
diuretics.

c. Sudden standing and prolonged standing are the precipitating

causes.

d. It is also associated with premonitory symptoms
(lightheadedness, nausea, etc.).

e. Treat with increased sodium intake and fluids. Consider
fludrocortisone if recurrent. Remember to treat other underlying
causes if identified (such as withdrawal of offending
medication).

2. Severe cerebrovascular disease
a. A rare cause of syncope.

b. A TIA involving the vertebrobasilar circulation may lead to
syncope ("drop attacks").

c. One practically never sees dizziness (or vertigo) in isolation
with vertebrobasilar insuficiency-there will always be other
deficits as well.

Quick HIT
If syncope occurs with exertion, assess for potentially life-
threatening causes such as HCM or AS.

Quick HIT
Syncope is uncommon in setting of a stroke, unless the
vertebrobasilar system is involved.

C. Diagnosis
1. First, attempt to rule out conditions that are life threatening (e.g.,
MI, hemorrhage, and arrhythmias) (Figure 1-16).

FIGURE
1-16 Syncope flowchart.

(From Linzer M, Yang EH, Estes NA III, et al. Clinical Guideline: Diagnosing syncope: Part 1:
Value of history, physical examination, and electrocardiography. Ann Intern Med
1997;126(12):989-966. Copyright  1997 American College of Physicians. All Rights
Reserved. Reprinted with the permission of American College of Physicians, Inc.)

Quick HIT
Evaluating Syncope
- The most important factors in decision making in syncope
patients are presence/absence of structural heart disease and
abnormal ECG.

1. The main goal is to differentiate between cardiac and
noncardiac etiologies, because the prognosis is poorest for those
with underlying heart disease.

## 2. History

a. Three key elements need to be determined: events before,
during, and after the syncopal episode.

b. Check the patient's medications-this is especially important in
elderly patients.

c. Seek reports from witnesses of the syncopal event.

3. Physical examination (priority given to cardiovascular system)
a. BP and pulse measurements in supine, sitting, and standing
positions (orthostatic vital signs)
b. Mental status (postictal state)
c. Murmurs (AS, HCM)

## 4. Diagnosis

a. ECG should be obtained in all patients with syncope, and may
identify life-threatening causes (ventricular tachycardia, other
arrhythmias, ischemia).

b. CBC, metabolic panel, and other labs based on the history and
physical may be appropriate.

c. Echocardiography if structural heart disease is suspected.

d. Additional diagnostic tests may be indicated, and include
ambulatory ECG monitoring, exercise testing, carotid sinus
massage, neurologic studies such as EEG or neurovascular
imaging, and electrophysiologic studies. Tilt-table testing was
frequently used to diagnose vasovagal syncope in the past, but
is now controversial.

e. Risk stratification tools (e.g., San Francisco Syncope Rule) can
help in the decision of whether or not to admit the patient to
the hospital for further evaluation, or if they can be discharged
home with follow-up.

Diseases of the
Pulmonary System 	2
Kelley Chuang
Obstructive Lung Diseases
```

### Source Excerpt: Syncope

_Source lines: 4329-4609._

```text
VI. SYNCOPE
A. Discussion. Syncope accounts for at least 1% to 2% of ED visits annually.

1. Definition. Transient loss of consciousness from which the patient has sponta-
neously recovered.

a. Presyncope is a poorly defined term referring to symptoms of weakness,
dizziness, or faintness, without complete loss of consciousness, which
resolve spontaneously. The term can also be applied to symptoms preceding
a full syncopal attack.

b. Patients presenting with loss of consciousness followed by a partial recovery
do not have true syncope, although the underlying mechanism may be
similar to several of the causes of syncope.

2. Causes. The mechanism of syncope, in the vast majority of cases, is a shortfall
in the supply of oxygen to the brain. This deficiency is usually due to disruption
of cerebral circulation rather than inadequate oxygenation of blood in the lungs.

a. Autonomic dysfunction. Adequate cerebral perfusion depends on adequate
venous return, adequate peripheral resistance (maintained by arteriolar con-
striction), and adequate cardiac output (maintained by cardiac inotropy and
chronotropy). Impairment of any one of these components of the circula-
tory system, which are largely under autonomic control, can cause a critical
fall in cerebral perfusion.

i. Vasovagal syncope usually occurs in the context of an emotionally dis-
turbing situation, such as extreme fear or injury. The patient may report
that the syncopal episode was preceded by nausea, warmth, dizziness,
or "roaring in the ears," and that the episode resolved rapidly once the
patient was placed in a recumbent position.

ii. Postural syncope
a) Benign postural syncope occurs in a percentage of healthy people
after prolonged standing. A typical scenario is syncope after pro-
longed standing during a religious ceremony. Patients are always
more susceptible to autonomic syncope while standing because the
head is the hydrostatically lowest pressure zone of the body in a
person who is standing erect.

b) Pathologic postural syncope differs from benign postural syncope
in that patients tend to be elderly and often have systemic disorders
responsible for central or peripheral neuropathies. Pathologic pos-
tural syncope usually occurs after the patient rises from a seated or
lying position. On examination, symptoms can often be reproduced
by asking the patient to stand up; orthostatic blood pressure mea-
surements are often abnormal. Medications are a common cause,
and a careful history will often illicit the source of the problem.

iii. Carotid hypersensitivity is associated with a history consistent with
inadvertent carotid stimulation, such as a tight collar or head turning.

iv. Pain-induced syncope is often associated with severe pain, especially
pain of a visceral origin (e.g., abdominal aortic aneurysm, ruptured
ectopic pregnancy).

v. Vasoactive drugs. A vast number of drugs have vasoactive effects (e.g.,
antihypertensives, sedative-hypnotics, opiates, neuroleptics, cholinergics).

b. Inadequate venous filling
i. Hypovolemia. Syncope can be caused by inadequate intravascular
volume, such as can occur with dehydration or hemorrhage. The
patient shows signs of orthostatic hypotension.

Quick 	HIT
Autonomic dysregulation
is the most common single
cause of identifiable syn-
cope. However, in most
cases of syncope the cause
is undetermined.

Quick 	HIT
A first episode of syncope is
unlikely to be attributable to
benign vasovagal causes in
patients older than 35 years.

52 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
ii. Mechanical obstruction of venous return to the heart (due to severe
mitral or tricuspid valve stenosis or a ball-valve thrombus, atrial myx-
oma, pulmonary embolus, or prosthetic valve malfunction) may cause
syncope.

iii. Situational causes are due to decreased venous return to the heart
from Valsalva, which is exacerbated by increased vagal cardiopressor
effects-for example, paroxysmal coughing ("tussive syncope"), void-
ing ("micturition syncope"), or defecation.

c. Inadequate pumping action of the heart
i. Cardiac conduction problems
a) Tachydysrhythmia, atrial or ventricular, is frequently associated with
a history of palpitations or previous episodes. Syncope due to resolved
ventricular tachycardia may be suggested by a prolonged QT interval
or the murmur of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (see X.B.3.b).

b) Bradydysrhythmia (usually characterized by a heart rate of less than
40 beats/min) can be due to injury to pacemaker cells or to the con-
duction system.

1) AV nodal block can cause syncope with no prodromal symp-
toms. Complete AV block, persistent or intermittent, that leads
to syncope is known as Stokes-Adams syndrome. The diagnosis
is suggested by the absence of prodrome and an ECG showing
bradycardia, new fascicular or Mobitz type II blocks, or bundle
branch block.

2) Sick sinus (bradycardia-tachycardia) syndrome can cause syn-
cope by sinus arrest following paroxysmal supraventricular tachy-
cardia. A history of prodromal palpitations should be sought.

ii. Ischemic heart disease can cause syncope by damaging the conduction
system or the myocardium. In patients older than 65 years, syncope be-
comes an increasingly common presentation of MI; these patients often
do not experience chest pain.

d. Obstruction of pulmonary blood flow due to pulmonary embolism, pulmo-
nary outflow tract obstruction, or chronic pulmonary hypertension should
be suspected in patients with increased respiratory rates, increased heart
rates, or decreased oxygenation.

e. Obstruction of cardiac outflow. Aortic outflow tract obstruction often
causes syncope associated with exertion.

i. Aortic stenosis can lead to syncope by both the mechanism of dimin-
ished arterial perfusion pressure and that of conduction system calcifi-
cation (leading to heart block and arrhythmias).

ii. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy can cause syncope via impairment of
aortic outflow, but most commonly, the syncope is related to ventricular
arrhythmias associated with the cardiomyopathy.

f. Disturbances of arterial circulation
i. Cerebrovascular insufficiency (due to embolic, atherosclerotic, or
thrombotic phenomena) usually causes focal neurologic deficits rather
than a loss of consciousness. Rarely, occlusion of the vertebrobasilar
artery can lead to dysfunction of the reticular activating system, causing
syncope.

ii. Subclavian steal is an unusual cause of syncope. It is suggested by syn-
cope associated with exertion involving the upper extremities. An arte-
rial blood pressure difference of 20 mm Hg or more is found between
measurements taken in the left and right arm.

g. Other causes
i. Hyperventilation is a common cause of presyncope and can cause
syncope. A history of anxiety or precipitating emotional stress should
be sought. Patients may complain of numbness; tingling in the lips,
face, or extremities; and spasms or dysfunction of the hands or feet.

A provocative test with the patient carefully monitored can be performed.

C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	53

Cardiovascular Emergencies
Pulmonary embolism must be carefully excluded in any patient in whom
hyperventilation is being considered as a cause of syncope.

ii. Intracerebral vascular catastrophe. Subarachnoid hemorrhage or the
sentinel bleed of a leaking saccular aneurysm can cause syncope, prob-
ably by autonomic mechanisms and cerebral vasospasm. However,
intracerebral vascular catastrophe usually leads to coma, not syncope.

Headache, photophobia, nausea, vomiting, neck pain, or meningeal
signs suggest the diagnosis. A careful search for cranial nerve abnor-
malities might reveal signs of aneurysmal nerve compression.

iii. Drugs can cause syncope by almost any of the mechanisms described.

Any drug that has autonomic, vascular, cardiac, or central nervous sys-
tem sedative or stimulant effects can cause or predispose to syncope.

iv. Psychogenic syncope is a diagnosis of exclusion that cannot be reli-
ably made with the time and resources available in the ED, especially
because psychiatric or psychologic processes can precipitate organic
causes of syncope.

B. Clinical features
1. History. The history of events surrounding the syncopal episode may suggest
the cause.

a. Palpitations, chest pain, or shortness of breath may suggest an arrhythmic
event, ischemia, or a pulmonary etiology.

b. A change in posture or prolonged standing immediately preceding the epi-
sode may reflect a postural cause or orthostatic syncope.

c. Emotional upsets usually precede vasovagal syncope or syncope caused by
hyperventilation.

d. Recent changes in medications as well as a current drug history may re-
veal drugs likely to precipitate syncope (e.g., # blockers, antiarrhythmic
agents, antidepressive agents, diuretics, other antihypertensive medications,
alcohol, cocaine).

e. The patient should be questioned regarding recent illnesses that may have
resulted in dehydration.

f. A past medical history of ischemic or valvular heart disease, pulmonary dis-
ease, or prior syncopal episodes may help determine the cause.

2. Physical examination findings. The physical examination must be focused on
the cardiovascular system to assess the probability of a life-threatening cause;
however, abnormal findings are usually absent.

a. Evaluation of vital signs with testing of blood pressure in all extremities and
orthostatic vitals is mandatory.

b. Rectal examination with Hemoccult (Beckman Coulter, Brea, CA) testing
may reveal gastrointestinal bleeding.

c. Examination of the extremities may reveal evidence of thrombophlebitis as
a source for pulmonary embolism.

d. Neurologic examination may reveal subtle findings suggestive of cerebrovas-
cular insufficiency.

C. Differential diagnoses
1. Hypoglycemia usually causes a gradual impairment of consciousness that is
not reversed until dextrose is administered. Hypoglycemia should be consid-
ered a reversible cause of coma rather than a cause of syncope.

2. Seizures are a cause of spontaneously reversible loss of consciousness and
must be distinguished from syncope. If, despite the clues listed below, the diag-
nosis is still unclear, the disposition should be based on the diagnosis of both
possible syncope and possible new-onset (atypical) seizure.

a. Seizures usually have a postictal period of impaired consciousness usually
lasting 10 minutes. In syncope, there is a rapid and complete return to a
clear sensorium.

b. Patients rarely go limp during seizures. In syncope, there is usually a com-
plete absence of motor activity. In the uncommon situation that transient
tonic-clonic activity is witnessed during a syncopal episode, it will be
54 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
described as following a short period of flaccid paralysis; in seizures, con-
vulsive motor activity occurs simultaneously with the loss of consciousness.

c. Most patients with seizures have a known history of the problem.

D. Evaluation. The cause of a given patient's syncope will not be found in 40% of pa-
tients despite extensive testing.

1. Electrocardiography. An ECG is warranted in all patients without an unequiv-
ocal noncardiac diagnosis, and cardiac monitoring should be initiated in the
ED in these patients.

2. Radiography. A chest radiograph is indicated in any patient with abnormal
vital signs, respiratory complaints, or suspected cardiac syncope.

3. Laboratory studies. A CBC and serum electrolyte panel may reveal an anemia
or electrolyte imbalance that may contribute to the cause of syncope.

4. Bedside echocardiography can be useful in revealing cardiac tamponade,
valvular abnormalities, abdominal aortic aneurysm, or ventricular wall motion
abnormalities.

E. Disposition

## 1. Admission (see Clinical Pearl 2-2)

a. Patients with an identified serious cause of syncope will be admitted for
urgent treatment or observation and monitoring.

b. In the case of patients with an indeterminate cause of syncope (by far the
largest group), clinical judgment is necessary to determine the likelihood of
serious underlying illness.

2. Discharge. Patients with a clearly benign cause for their syncope can be discharged
from the ED to follow-up with their primary physician within several days.
```
